title: Estimation of conserved charges for a one dimensional system with inhomogeneous hopping
abstract: Quantum integrability in a system is characterized by a large number of conserved charges. However, finding the conserved charges in a generic quantum system is highly challenging. The integrable matrix theory provides a unified framework to obtain the conserved charges in a certain class of systems. We demonstrate this framework in case of a single-particle system on a 1D finite-sized lattice with inhomogeneous nearest neighbor hopping to study the statistical properties of the system across its chaotic--integrable crossover from the perspective of the conserved charges. The eigenspectrum of our random matrix model is studied first. We then estimate the conserved charges and find their properties throughout the chaotic to integrable transition of the system. We calculate the number of conserved charges across this crossover and observe that it is nearly equal to the size of the system at its integrable limit. Our result suggests that the number of conserved charges, estimated using the integrable matrix theory, can be a measure of quantum integrability.
summary: This paper analyzes a 1D random matrix model with inhomogeneous hopping to study the transition from chaotic to integrable quantum behavior. Using the Integrable Matrix Theory, the authors estimate conserved charges and show that the count of these charges provides a quantitative measure of integrability across this crossover. This work links spectral statistics and localization measures directly to fundamental concepts in quantum integrability.
main_problem: The study investigates how conserved charges characterize the crossover between chaotic and integrable regimes in a one-dimensional quantum system.
main_result: The number of estimated conserved charges, derived from the Integrable Matrix Theory (IMT), serves as a quantitative measure for the degree of quantum integrability across this transition.
method: The authors employ the IMT framework to estimate conserved charges by analyzing recurrence relations involving matrix elements and eigenvalues of the system's Hamiltonian.
model_or_system: The model is a single-particle system on a 1D lattice with inhomogeneous nearest neighbor hopping, described by an IH random matrix ensemble. The Hamiltonian depends on a tunable parameter $\gamma$ that controls the transition between regimes.
key_observables: Number of conserved charges ($n$), spectral statistics (Nearest Neighbor Spacing Distribution $P(s)$), Inverse Participation Ratio ($	ext{IPR}$), and Fractal Dimension ($D_q$).
important_parameters: The tuning parameter $\gamma$ controls the crossover, ranging from chaotic to integrable limits. The system size $N$ is also a critical factor.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on extending the IMT framework to systems with inhomogeneous hopping and assumes that the number of conserved charges can be reliably estimated via convergence rates ($\eta$) or slope analysis ($\Delta\gamma$).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the Density of States collapsing onto the Wigner semicircle law, transitions in spectral statistics between GOE/Poisson limits, increasing eigenstate localization ($D_q$), and the dependence of $n/N$ on $\gamma$.
paper_structure: The paper first introduces the IH model and its Hamiltonian structure. It then details the methodology using IMT to estimate conserved charges by analyzing convergence rates ($\eta$). Finally, it analyzes how these charges, alongside spectral statistics and localization measures, track the crossover from chaotic ($\gamma 	o 0$) to integrable ($\gamma > 1$) behavior.
why_interesting: The connection drawn between counting conserved quantities (a hallmark of integrability) and measurable spectral/localization properties provides a powerful diagnostic tool for understanding quantum dynamics in complex systems, relevant to many-body physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Density of states (a) for diﬀerent values of γ ≥1 for 5000 samples of system size N = 500. All the plots fol- low Wigner semicircle law. (b) shows the size dependence of density of states at γ = 1 for 5000 samples of system sizes N = 100, 200, 500, 1000 and 2000. If x-axis scales as E → E √

FIG. 2. Nearest neighbor spacing distribution P(s) for 5000 samples of system size N = 500. (a) depicts P(s) for 0 < γ < 1. It transits from GOE (at γ ∼0.34) to near Poisson limit as γ increases. (b) shows P(s) for γ > 1. It comes back towards GOE limit with a further increase of γ and overlaps with Wigner distribution for γ ∼2. P(s) does not change any more if γ increases further (not shown for clarity).

FIG. 3. Size dependence of ensemble averaged ratio ⟨˜r⟩of nearest neighbor spacing for 5000 samples of system size N = 100, 200, 500, 1000, 2000. As γ increases from zero, it goes towards the Poisson limit touching GOE limit at γ ≃0.29 for N = 2000 and γ ≃0.42 for N = 100. γ = 0 is a critical point with ⟨˜r⟩= 0.65 lying the statistics between GUE and GSE limits. ⟨˜r⟩attains maximum integrability at γ ≃1 for systems of all sizes N. But larger ones become more integrable with ⟨˜r⟩→Poisson limit. It again moves toward GOE statistics and superposes with it around γ ≳2. Here also larger systems attain at smaller values of γ. All the curves again cross with each other at γ ≃1.25 with ⟨˜r⟩= 0.44.

FIG. 4. Size dependence of the fractal dimension D(N) 2 for 5000 samples of system size N = 100, 200, 500, 1000, 2000. At γ = −0.25, the system is maximally delocalized with D(N) 2 → 1. At this point, delocalization increases, as N increases. The eigenstates become multifractal with increasing γ and they reach a complete localization D(N) 2 = 0 around γ ≃2. As the system size N increases, localisation is attained at a smaller value of γ. With a further increment of γ, the eigenstates remain localized. All the curves for any system size intersect at γ ≃0.57 with D(N) 2 ≃0.38.

Fig. 4 for q = 2 only. It shows that with increasing γ, the localization in eigenstates increases and they become completely localized around γ ≃2 for any system size. The size dependence in Fig. 4 depicts that eigenstates of larger systems localize at smaller γ, suggesting that for a system with N →∞, localization (D2 = 0) is attained at γ →1. The eigenstates remain localized for further increment of γ, i.e., D(N) 2 never goes again towards the Wigner-Dyson limit, unlike the spectral properties of our IH ensemble.
